explanation: The specification requires that when the sensors have exceeded their limits, and the pilot is NOT in control (i.e., not standby), and system support is indicated without external failures (supported and no apfail), then the autopilot latches (enters) the pullup maneuver. This is naturally captured by a global property: 'If limits & !standby & supported & !apfail, then pullup.' This uses the G (always) operator to ensure the property holds at all times. Any time all the specified conditions are met, pullup must also be true.

This property encodes that if sensor limits are exceeded (limits==True), and both the system is supported without failures (supported==True and apfail==False), and the pilot is not in control (standby==False), then it is always the case that autopilot pullup (pullup==True) is latched (initiated and maintained). The latching effect can be interpreted as requiring that in every state, under these preconditions, pullup must be True. Thus, using the template for 'always if (cond) then (effect)' with the proper boolean conditions.

This LTL property expresses that whenever the sensors exceed limits (limits is true), and the system is healthy (supported is true), there are no failures (apfail is false), and the pilot is not in control (standby is false), then the autopilot must latch the pullup (pullup becomes true) globally. The formula ensures that these conditions, when true, always lead to the pullup maneuver being initiated, specifically matching the requirements that it should only happen if the pilot is not in control, the system is supported, and there is no external failure.

This LTL property states that whenever the pilot is not in control (not standby), the system is supported (supported is true), there is no autopilot failure (not apfail), and the sensors indicate a faulty condition (limits), then the autopilot shall latch the pullup maneuver (pullup becomes true). The template expresses that globally, if all these conditions hold together, then the pullup must be true. This directly matches the requirement to latch a pullup under those safety conditions.

The property states that whenever (at all times) the pilot is NOT in control (not standby), and the system is supported (supported true), and there is no failure (not apfail), and the sensors are indicating a fault (limits is true), then the autopilot must latch (immediately go into) the pullup (MANEUVER) state. The implication is formalized as: G(((not standby) & supported & (not apfail) & limits) -> pullup). This implements the requirement in the natural language statement: when the conditions, including not being in standby, being system-supported and without failures, and sensors exceeded limits, are all true, the pullup must be latched.

This LTL property expresses that whenever the sensors are exceeding their limits, and (1) the pilot is not in control (not standby), and (2) the system is healthy and supported (supported is true and there is no apfail), then the autopilot must latch a pullup. The trigger is (limits & !standby & supported & !apfail), and the safety latching action is (pullup). The globally operator ensures that this property must hold at all times.

The property requires that whenever the pilot is not in control (not standby), the system is supported (supported is true), and there are no failures (not apfail), and the sensor is indicating limits (limits is true), then the autopilot must latch (go into) pullup (pullup becomes true). This matches as a global response property, using a conjunction of all these conditions as the trigger, and pullup as the required response.

The requirement states that when the system is supported (supported is True), there are no failures (apfail is False), and the pilot is not in control (standby is False), then if sensor limits are exceeded (limits is True), the autopilot pullup must latch (pullup is True). This is best modeled as a safety property: globally, whenever the conjunction of the pilot not being in control, the system being supported, and there being no apfail, and sensor limits is True, then pullup must become True. The LTL template for 'Globally, if (cond1 and cond2 and cond3) then cond4' fits well. Here, bool_exp1 = '(supported & (! apfail) & (! standby) & limits)', and bool_exp3 = 'pullup'. The duration N_DURATION is not relevant for G, so it's set but not used.

The requirement states that when the sensors indicate a faulty condition ('limits' is True), and the pilot is NOT in control ('!standby'), and the system is supported and not failed ('supported & !apfail'), the autopilot must enter or latch the pullup mode ('pullup' becomes True). 

This can be translated as: always (globally), whenever all three preconditions ('limits' is True, 'standby' is False, and 'supported & !apfail') hold, then 'pullup' must also be True. This fits a template enforcing that when all three Boolean expressions (here combined as 'limits & !standby & supported & !apfail') are True, 'pullup' is required to be True at the same time. This is handled by template 40: G((bool_exp1 & bool_exp2) -> bool_exp3).

The requirement describes that when sensor limits are exceeded (limits is true), the system health is supported (supported is true), there are no failures (not apfail), and the pilot is not in control (not standby), then an autopilot pullup should eventually be latched (pullup is true). To capture the combination of all the preconditions, I combine them: (limits & supported & (!apfail) & (!standby)), and require that whenever this is true, eventually pullup will be true. This matches with the template 'G (bool_exp1 -> (F bool_exp3))', where bool_exp1 is the conjunction of triggering conditions, and bool_exp3 is 'pullup'. N_DURATION is not strictly necessary, so we choose the infinite-time version.
